questions.q: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction is written \( \varepsilon = -N\dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} \). What quantity does the induced EMF depend on?
options: The magnitude of the magnetic flux through the coil
The rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil
The strength of the magnetic field alone
The total area of the coil alone
questions.explanation: The EMF is proportional to the RATE of change of flux, not the flux itself. A huge but unchanging flux gives zero rate of change and hence zero EMF; a small flux changing fast can give a large EMF. 'Change is everything.'

questions.q: Why does a strong but stationary magnet inside a coil induce no EMF?
options: The field is too uniform to induce anything
The flux is constant, so its rate of change is zero
The coil resistance cancels the induced EMF
The magnet must touch the wire to induce an EMF
questions.explanation: A stationary magnet gives a constant flux through the coil. Since nothing is changing, \( \Delta\Phi/\Delta t = 0 \), so by Faraday's law the EMF is zero — no matter how strong the magnet is. This is the core 'why change is everything' point.

questions.q: Using the 'swing the BAT' hook, which list correctly gives the three ways to change the flux through a coil?
options: Change the battery, the amplitude, and the temperature
Change the field strength B, the area A, and the angle \( \theta \)
Change the brightness, the acceleration, and the torque
Change the field strength B only — area and angle do not affect flux
questions.explanation: From \( \Phi = BA\cos\theta \), changing any of B, A, or \( \theta \) changes the flux. Move a magnet (B), slide a rod on rails to change the enclosed area (A), or rotate the coil to change the angle (\( \theta \)) — all run through the identical induction machinery.

questions.q: A rectangular loop \( 0.25\,\mathrm{m} \times 0.30\,\mathrm{m} \) (single turn) takes \( 2.0\,\mathrm{s} \) to fully enter a field of \( 0.66\,\mathrm{T} \) perpendicular to its plane. What is the magnitude of the induced EMF?
If the loop in the previous question instead had 200 turns (same field, area, and time), the induced EMF would be:
options: Unchanged, because the flux change is the same
200 times larger, about \( 5.0\,\mathrm{V} \)
200 times smaller, about \( 0.00012\,\mathrm{V} \)
Doubled, about \( 0.050\,\mathrm{V} \)
questions.explanation: Each turn encloses the same changing flux and so has the same induced EMF; the turns are in series, so their EMFs add. With \( N = 200 \), the EMF is \( 200 \times 0.025 \approx 5.0\,\mathrm{V} \) — same flux change, more turns, more EMF.

questions.q: A circular loop of radius \( 0.15\,\mathrm{m} \) in a constant \( 0.30\,\mathrm{T} \) field (perpendicular, single turn) is squashed to radius \( 0.070\,\mathrm{m} \) in \( 0.13\,\mathrm{s} \). Even though B never changed, an EMF appears. Why?
options: The field secretly changed direction
The area shrank, so the flux \( \Phi = BA \) decreased
Resistance heating created a voltage
No EMF can appear if B is constant
questions.explanation: Flux is \( BA\cos\theta \), not just B. The area fell as the loop shrank, so the flux fell even with B fixed. Constant field does not mean constant flux — this is 'lever A' of the BAT, giving \( |\varepsilon| \approx 0.13\,\mathrm{V} \) here.

questions.q: When a magnet is pushed into a solenoid, which is the correct three-step chain explaining the result?
options: The magnet directly creates a current in the coil
Flux changes → an EMF is induced (Faraday) → a current flows if the circuit is complete
A current flows → this creates an EMF → which changes the flux
The strong field creates a large flux → the large flux creates a current
questions.explanation: The correct chain is 'Flux, EMF, Current, if Closed': the moving magnet changes the flux; the changing flux induces an EMF; the EMF drives a current only if the coil forms a complete circuit. Saying 'the magnet makes a current' collapses the chain and loses marks.

questions.q: A coil is in an open circuit (a switch is open) when the flux through it changes. What happens?
options: No EMF is induced because there is no circuit
An EMF is induced, but no current flows because the circuit is not complete
Both an EMF and a current appear as normal
The flux change is cancelled by the open switch
questions.explanation: EMF and current must be kept strictly distinct. The induced EMF exists whether or not the circuit is closed — it is a voltage across the coil's ends. A current only flows if there is a closed conducting loop. 'No circuit, so no EMF' is wrong; there is an EMF, just no current.

questions.q: A student claims a solid metal plate cannot have any current induced in it because, unlike a coil, it has no wire loops. This claim is:
options: Correct — induction requires defined wire loops
Incorrect — a changing flux induces an EMF in bulk metal, driving circulating eddy currents
Correct — only solenoids can be induced in
Incorrect, but only because the plate is magnetic
questions.explanation: Induction needs only a conductor and a changing flux, not discrete wires. A changing flux through the plate induces an EMF that drives swirling closed-loop currents through the solid metal — eddy currents. By Lenz's law these oppose the motion (a braking force).

questions.q: A single square loop of side \( 0.20\,\mathrm{m} \), plane perpendicular to the field, experiences a field rising steadily from \( 0 \) to \( 0.50\,\mathrm{T} \) in \( 0.10\,\mathrm{s} \). What is the magnitude of the induced EMF?
questions.explanation: \( A = 0.20^2 = 0.04\,\mathrm{m^2} \). With the field perpendicular, \( \Phi_f = 0.50 \times 0.04 = 0.02\,\mathrm{Wb} \), \( \Phi_i = 0 \), so \( \Delta\Phi = 0.02\,\mathrm{Wb} \). \( |\varepsilon| = 1 \times 0.02/0.10 = 0.2\,\mathrm{V} \).
